headline: Iranian Missile Strikes Are Costing Big Oil Billions in Lost Revenue
summary: Damaged energy infrastructure is likely to take years to come back online, but a surge in oil-and-gas prices is helping to offset some of the lost production for now.

headline: Chevron Weighs Gulf Damage Costs Against Higher Oil Price Support
summary: Iranian missile strikes have caused significant and lasting damage to Gulf energy infrastructure, disrupting output and exports. Major oil companies, including Chevron, are facing billions in lost revenue tied to damaged facilities and supply interruptions. Oil prices have surged as global markets react to the supply shock, creating both operational challenges and potential short term revenue benefits for NYSE:CVX. For Chevron, this infrastructure shock lands at a time when the stock is...

headline: Energy: What to watch at CERAWeek amid Iran war, rising oil prices
summary: World energy leaders and officials will convene at the CERAWeek conference starting March 23, which is annually hosted by S&P Global and has been dubbed the "Davos of energy." Yahoo Finance Breaking News Reporter Jake Conley speaks with Market Catalysts Host Julie Hyman about the topics that will be tackled at CERAWeek — not just the recent rise in oil prices (CL=F, BZ=F) in relation to Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz amid escalating conflicts — as well as the top executives they will be interviewing at the event.

content.title: Energy: What to watch at CERAWeek amid Iran war, rising oil prices
content.description:

headline: What energy leaders are saying about oil prices, Iran at CERAWeek 2026
summary: As the Iran war stretches into its fourth week and oil prices (CL=F, BZ=F) remain volatile, the biggest names in the energy industry are convening in Houston for S&P Global's CERAWeek conference. Yahoo Finance Anchor Julie Hyman and Breaking News Reporter Jake Conley recap the geopolitical commentary they are already hearing from executives at the event, forecasts around oil prices, and Iran's ability to maintain a "high enough" threat level to keep the Strait of Hormuz closed.

headline: Chevron CEO Wirth on Oil Markets: They're Uncertain and Volatile
summary: Chevron CEO Mike Wirth talks about the impact of the war in Iran on oil prices. "They're uncertain, unpredictable, they're volatile. He speaks during a discussion at CERA Week in Houston.

headline: Chevron CEO: More Groundwork Needed to Boost Venezuelan Oil Production
summary: Chevron boosted oil production in Venezuela early this year, but big investments in the country’s beleaguered oil sector will require more changes in its laws, Chevron CEO Mike Wirth says.  Venezuela earlier this year rewrote key hydrocarbon laws to favor private investment, and Trump officials have spoken in recent weeks with U.S. oil executives about the need to invest more in the country to secure Western Hemisphere fuel supply lines while the Iran conflict keeps the Strait of Hormuz closed.  There is “uncertainty in the law that I think could be tightened up,” Wirth said at the S&P Global CERAWeek energy conference in downtown Houston Monday, adding that tens of billions in investments would be needed to push Venezuela’s oil production to the levels seen a decade ago.

content.title: Chevron CEO: More Groundwork Needed to Boost Venezuelan Oil Production
content.summary: Chevron boosted oil production in Venezuela early this year, but big investments in the country’s beleaguered oil sector will require more changes in its laws, Chevron CEO Mike Wirth says.  Venezuela earlier this year rewrote key hydrocarbon laws to favor private investment, and Trump officials have spoken in recent weeks with U.S. oil executives about the need to invest more in the country to secure Western Hemisphere fuel supply lines while the Iran conflict keeps the Strait of Hormuz closed.  There is “uncertainty in the law that I think could be tightened up,” Wirth said at the S&P Global CERAWeek energy conference in downtown Houston Monday, adding that tens of billions in investments would be needed to push Venezuela’s oil production to the levels seen a decade ago.

headline: Oil Markets Tighter Than Investors Realize, Chevron CEO Says
summary: The world’s physical markets for oil and fuel are tighter than financial investors in energy futures realize, Chevron CEO Mike Wirth said, signaling that prices could rise further as long as the Strait of Hormuz remains closed.  “The markets are trading on some scant information and perception,” Wirth said at the S&P Global CERAWeek conference in Houston Monday.  “Physical prices and physical supplies would reflect a tighter market than I think the forward curve reflects.”

content.title: Oil Markets Tighter Than Investors Realize, Chevron CEO Says
content.summary: The world’s physical markets for oil and fuel are tighter than financial investors in energy futures realize, Chevron CEO Mike Wirth said, signaling that prices could rise further as long as the Strait of Hormuz remains closed.  “The markets are trading on some scant information and perception,” Wirth said at the S&P Global CERAWeek conference in Houston Monday.  “Physical prices and physical supplies would reflect a tighter market than I think the forward curve reflects.”

headline: Chevron CEO says oil market underpricing Hormuz closure impact
summary: Investing.com -- The oil futures market has not fully accounted for the supply disruption caused by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, Chevron CEO Mike Wirth said Monday.

content.title: Chevron CEO says oil market underpricing Hormuz closure impact
content.summary: Investing.com -- The oil futures market has not fully accounted for the supply disruption caused by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, Chevron CEO Mike Wirth said Monday.

headline: Chevron’s CEO says oil prices are still too low—and the effects of the Strait of Hormuz closure are not ‘fully priced in’
summary: “Physical supply changes don’t respond immediately,” said Wirth. “Even when strait reopens at some point, it will take time.”

content.title: Chevron’s CEO says oil prices are still too low—and the effects of the Strait of Hormuz closure are not ‘fully priced in’
content.summary: “Physical supply changes don’t respond immediately,” said Wirth. “Even when strait reopens at some point, it will take time.”
